itineraries.title: Uzbekistan: From Tashkent to the Fergana Valley Along the Silk Road
itineraries.subtitle: Timurid capitals, Tian Shan mountains and the kingdom of ikat silk
itineraries.notes: The Afrosiyob train (Spanish Talgo) connects Tashkent–Samarkand in 2h10 and Samarkand–Bukhara in 1h30 at 250 km/h. Book your tickets several weeks in advance during high season (tickets often sell out months ahead). Margilan's Kumtepa bazaar is open only on Thursdays and Sundays.

visits.text: Chorsu Bazaar has anchored the heart of Tashkent's old city since the Middle Ages — its enormous turquoise dome is impossible to miss. Under the central cupola, dried fruits, spices, fresh bread and ikat silks spill across hundreds of stalls. Set aside a good two hours to take it all in at a comfortable pace.

The Khast Imam complex brings together two 16th-century madrasas and the Hazroti Imam Friday Mosque, whose 54-metre minarets pierce the Tashkent skyline. The complex library houses one of the oldest Qurans in the world, dating back to the 7th century.

activities.text: Ride the Tashkent Metro as you would visit a museum: a Soviet legacy from the 1970s and 80s, each station is decorated in its own distinct style. Get off at Alisher Navoi for its tiled vaulted ceilings, at Pakhtakor for its floral mosaics, and at Kosmonavtlar for its ceramic cosmonauts.

As evening falls, Amir Timur Square is worth a stroll — the illuminated fountains and the equestrian statue of Tamerlane at the centre of the roundabout make for a striking scene. The square is flanked by buildings from three distinct eras: the Soviet Brutalist Hotel Uzbekistan, the Timurid museum under its turquoise dome, and the Broadway pedestrian alley leading to Independence Square.

visits.text: Eighty kilometres northeast of Tashkent, the Chimgan Mountains form part of the western Tian Shan range. Greater Chimgan peaks at 3,309 metres; Little Chimgan, manageable as a day hike, tops out at 2,118 metres. On a clear day from the summit, the Charvak Reservoir glitters like a turquoise stone set among the ridgelines.

activities.text: Hike Little Chimgan (2,118 m): the ascent starts from the village via the Pisochniy Pass (1,825 m), roughly 3–4 hours round trip. The slope is sustained on open terrain with little shade — start early. The panorama from the top stretches across Greater Chimgan, the Chatkal ridge and Lake Charvak.

practical_info.equipement: Good hiking shoes, plenty of water

activities.text: Optionally, take the Beldersay cable car (10–18 USD) for sweeping views over Greater Chimgan. The Charvak Reservoir — an artificial lake created in 1970 by a 168-metre dam on the Chirchik River — makes a pleasant stop on the way back.

visits.text: The Timurid Museum is housed in a crown-shaped building erected in 1999 to mark the 660th anniversary of Amir Temur. Its sculpted doors and stained-glass windows are already remarkable before you step inside. The collection features scale models of the great monuments, armour and artefacts from the Timurid era.

The Museum of Applied Arts occupies the former palace of Russian diplomat Alexander Polovtsev — a striking blend of Russian and Oriental architecture. Silk embroideries, ceramics, musical instruments and carved woodwork offer a comprehensive survey of craft traditions across Uzbekistan's regions.

activities.text: Book your Afrosiyob train ticket for the following morning to Samarkand. The Spanish Talgo covers the distance in 2h10 at 250 km/h. Reserve several weeks in advance — seats go quickly, especially in high season.

practical_info.conseil: Book 2–4 weeks ahead during high season

visits.text: Registan Square brings together three madrasas built between 1420 and 1660: Ulugh Beg (the oldest), Sher-Dor with its lions above the portal, and Tilya-Kori whose mosque ceiling mimics a gold-leaf dome. A UNESCO World Heritage Site, this is the defining image of Samarkand.

The Gur-e-Amir mausoleum shelters beneath its ribbed blue dome the tombs of Tamerlane, his sons and his grandson Ulugh Beg. Its early 15th-century cupola became the template for all Mughal architecture in India, right up to the Taj Mahal. A slab of black jade marks the exact resting place of the conqueror.

activities.text: Siab Bazaar, behind the Bibi-Khanym Mosque, is the living market of the old city. Samarkand melons are celebrated across the country — taste them early in the morning, before the tour groups arrive.

The Bibi-Khanym Mosque (1404), built to celebrate Tamerlane's return from his Indian campaign, was once among the largest in the Islamic world. Its sheer scale still impresses, centuries and earthquakes notwithstanding.

visits.text: Shah-i-Zinda — the Tomb of the Living King — is a narrow avenue lined with more than twenty mausoleums spanning the 11th to the 19th century. The finest date from the 14th and 15th centuries: deep-blue tilework, calligraphic inscriptions, carved stalactites. It is said that Qusam ibn Abbas, cousin of the Prophet, came here to preach Islam in the 7th century. Dress modestly. Legend holds that anyone who counts the same number of steps going up and coming down will have their wish granted.

Ulugh Beg's observatory (15th c.) has almost nothing left above ground — only the pit of a giant sextant with a 40-metre radius, buried in the hillside. Ulugh Beg, the scholar-sultan, charted more than 1,000 stars here with remarkable precision for his era. The adjacent museum traces his scientific legacy.

activities.text: The Afrosiyab Museum stands on the hill of the ancient Sogdian city (8th century BC). Its centrepiece is a 7th-century fresco depicting King Varkhuman receiving ambassadors from China, India and Korea — one of the rare pre-Islamic frescoes still visible in Central Asia.

visits.text: The Poi-Kalon complex gathers three monuments: the Kalon Minaret (1127, 47.5 metres), which legend says was the only structure spared by Genghis Khan, the Kalon Mosque with a capacity of 12,000 worshippers, and the Mir-i-Arab Madrasa (1535), still active today with 120 students. Take time to sit facing the minaret as the day winds down.

Lyabi-Hauz, a pool dug in 1620, is ringed by mulberry trees planted at the same time and three historic buildings: the Kukeldash Madrasa (1568), the Nadir Divan-Begi Madrasa and a Sufi khanaka. This is the beating heart of Bukhara, with café terraces and a statue of Nasreddin on his donkey.

The Ark Citadel, a fortress-residence of the emirs since the 5th century, towers over the square from a height of 20 metres. Palace, prison and place of execution: the entire history of the Emirate of Bukhara is written into its walls.

activities.text: Wander between the three 16th-century trading domes (taks) — jewellers, hatmakers, money changers — that punctuate the historic bazaar's main axis. Their architecture remains intact beneath the silks and souvenirs. The Samanid Mausoleum (10th century), in the nearby park, is the oldest monument in the city: its intricate fired-brick work creates mesmerising patterns of light and shadow.

visits.text: Rishtan (25 km from Fergana) is the world capital of blue and white ceramics. Local artisans use a mineral glaze that produces a cobalt blue impossible to replicate industrially — a technique dating back to the 12th century, inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list. In family workshops, you can watch the wheel turning, the brush painting and the firing in brick kilns.

activities.text: Back in Fergana, the regional museum offers useful context before Margilan: the history of the valley, Rishtan ceramic pieces and examples of ikat silks displayed side by side.

visits.text: The Yodgorlik factory (meaning 'souvenir'), founded in 1972, is the only manufacturer in the world where ikat silk is produced entirely by hand, from cocoon to finished fabric. Here you can watch women unreeling cocoons in boiling water, dyeing threads using the ikat resist technique, then weaving on wooden hand looms. 450 employees, 6,000 metres of fabric per month. The guided tour is free.
The Said Akhmad-Khoja Madrasa (19th c.) now houses Margilan's Craft Development Centre: the former student cells are occupied by master weavers of atlas and adras textiles.

activities.text: Kumtepa Bazaar, 5 km west of the town centre, opens only on Thursdays and Sundays. Men in chapan robes negotiate rolls of atlas and ikat fabric over pots of tea, with not a tourist in sight. Arrive between 8am and 11am for peak atmosphere.

visits.text: A final half-day in Tashkent before your flight home. If you haven't yet explored the Museum of Applied Arts (the former Polovtsev Palace) or strolled along Broadway Alley, now is the time. Otherwise, head back to Chorsu Bazaar to pick up spices and almond nougat to bring home.

activities.text: Sample street food and browse the market stalls
Explore the city and prepare for the train journey

seo.h1_title: Uzbekistan: From Tashkent to the Fergana Valley Along the Silk Road
seo.intro_paragraph: Board the Afrosiyob, Uzbekistan's high-speed train thundering along at 250 km/h, and connect the three great Timurid cities in just a few days. Samarkand with its blue domes rising above the Registan, Bukhara with its Kalon Minaret standing nine centuries tall, Tashkent with its Soviet metro-museum — three faces of a country that has kept its medieval splendour alive and breathing. To the east, the Fergana Valley adds another chapter: the ikat silk weavers of Margilan, heirs to a craft tradition 2,000 years in the making.
seo.seo_hook: Samarkand is just 2h10 from Tashkent by train, Bukhara only 1h30 from Samarkand: Uzbekistan is now within reach at high speed, yet its ancient monuments will stop you in your tracks.
featured_list: Samarkand: the Registan and its three UNESCO-listed Timurid madrasas
Bukhara: the Kalon Minaret of 1127, the only monument spared by Genghis Khan
Tashkent: the Soviet metro and Chorsu Bazaar, two centuries in a single neighbourhood
Chimgan: hiking to the Little Peak (2,118 m) with views over Lake Charvak
Margilan: the Yodgorlik factory, the world's only fully handmade ikat silk manufacturer

places_taglines.UZ::tashkent: Where a medieval bazaar, a Soviet metro turned open-air museum, and Tamerlane's grand square converge at the crossroads of Central Asia.
places_taglines.UZ::chimgan: A Tian Shan mountain escape just 80 km from Tashkent — hike Little Chimgan (2,118 m) and look out over the turquoise waters of Lake Charvak.
places_taglines.UZ::samarkand: Three madrasas around the Registan, Tamerlane's ribbed blue tomb at Gur-e-Amir, and the dazzling tiled avenue of Shah-i-Zinda — the soul of the Silk Road.
places_taglines.UZ::bukhara: The 1127 Kalon Minaret that outlasted Genghis Khan, a labyrinth of trading domes, and the mulberry-shaded pool of Lyabi-Hauz — a city frozen in amber.
places_taglines.UZ::fergana: Gateway to the fertile valley, where the cobalt blues of Rishtan pottery and the shimmering silks of Margilan have been crafted for over a thousand years.
places_taglines.UZ::margilan: The world capital of ikat silk: the entirely hand-operated Yodgorlik factory and the Kumtepa bazaar, alive only on Thursdays and Sundays.

highlights: Best season: April–May and September–October, with mild temperatures and clear skies. Summers are scorching (up to 40°C in July–August); while the heat weighs heavily in cities, the mountains around Chimgan stay cooler and are well suited to summer hiking.
The Afrosiyob train (Spanish Talgo) connects Tashkent–Samarkand in 2h10 and Samarkand–Bukhara in 1h30 at 250 km/h — book several weeks ahead, as seats sell out fast.
Samarkand is one of the oldest cities in the world (around 2,750 years old); its historic centre has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2001.
Margilan is the world's third-largest silk producer and the only place where khan-atlas (royal silk) is still woven entirely by hand using techniques over 2,000 years old.
Bukhara's Kalon Minaret (1127) stands 47.5 metres tall with foundations reaching 13 metres deep — which is precisely why it survived both earthquakes and the Mongol invasion.
Margilan's Kumtepa Bazaar is open only on Thursdays and Sundays: plan your arrival accordingly.
